## **About the book** 

"India 2020: A Vision for the New Millennium" by A.P.J. Abdul Kalam, co-authored with Y.S. Rajan, is a compelling blueprint for transforming India into a developed nation by the year 2020. Through the lens of a revered 

scientist-turned-statesman, the book offers a holistic and pragmatic roadmap that transcends mere economic growth to encompass human development, technological innovation, and sustainable practices. It delves into the untapped potential of India's natural resources, the prowess of its youth, and the unyielding spirit of its citizens. With strategic insights and actionable recommendations, the narrative implores every Indian to take an active role in the nation-building process. Engaged readers will find themselves inspired by the vision of a vibrant, prosperous, and equitable India, urging them to contribute to this grand, collective mission. 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

Scan to Download 

## **About the author** 

A.P.J. Abdul Kalam, also known as the "Missile Man of India," was an esteemed Indian aerospace scientist and the 11th President of India, serving from 2002 to 2007. Born on October 15, 1931, in Rameswaram, Tamil Nadu, Kalam rose from humble beginnings to become a leading figure in India's space and missile development programs, particularly through his work with the Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) and the Defence Research and Development Organisation (DRDO). Renowned for his pivotal role in India's civilian space program and military missile systems, he was instrumental in the successful Pokhran-II nuclear tests in 1998, which established India as a nuclear power. Beyond his scientific achievements, Kalam was a beloved educator and author, inspiring millions through his writings and speeches, emphasizing the importance of dreaming big, education, and national development. His book "India 2020" reflects his vision and aspirations for India's progress, aiming to transform the nation into a developed country by the year 2020.

## **Can India Become a Developed Country?** 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

This chapter explores the fundamental question of India's potential to evolve into a developed nation. Key indicators of development, such as the wealth of the nation, prosperity of its citizens, and global standing, are analyzed. Traditional economic metrics like GNP, GDP, and per capita income are noted, but the chapter emphasizes that such numbers can mask human suffering and inequality. 

## **Human Development Indicators** 

Several parameters are mentioned to assess the well-being of people, including nutrition, life expectancy, sanitation, healthcare access, and education. The chapter invokes Gandhi's principle that true development must alleviate poverty and erase the tears of the downtrodden, suggesting that uplifting the poorest remains an essential goal. 

## **Challenges of Development** 

The author reflects on the initial post-independence optimism versus current cynicism regarding India's ability to become a developed nation. Notably, it is emphasized that achieving developed status must not come at the expense of the 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

upliftment of all citizens. 

## **Technological Imperatives and Strategic Strengths** 

A forward-looking analysis suggests that India must leverage its technological competencies to secure economic advancement and national security. The importance of self-reliance in defense and technological areas is highlighted as critical for India to navigate complex global challenges. 

## **Economic Vision for 2020** 

Kalam outlines a "Technology Vision" integrated with economic and social dimensions aiming for significant GDP growth. Projections for India’s GDP, per capita income, income distribution, and poverty levels are made for 2020, while recognizing potential pitfalls due to economic turmoil in Southeast Asia and evolving global competition. 

## **Role of Education and Human Resources** 

The text discusses plans to strengthen social and economic opportunities through education, health care, and skill development. It asserts that improvements in literacy and 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

urban-rural integration will cultivate a more prosperous society. 

## **Responding to Global Dynamics** 

Kalam warns about selective economic warfare tactics from developed countries aiming to suppress emerging economies like India. The necessity for India to develop its own technologies while engaging positively with the world is emphasized. 

## **Envisioning Beyond 2020** 

The chapter concludes with a vision for continued progress past 2020, focusing on technological advancements, economic strength, social equity, and strategic foresight. It calls for strong leadership and collective action from the nation’s citizens to meet these challenges head-on.

## **Example** 

Key Point:The importance of uplifting the poorest citizens in the journey towards becoming a developed nation. 

Example:Imagine walking through your local community, observing neighborhoods where families struggle to afford basic necessities while others thrive. This stark contrast prompts you to think how crucial it is for India's journey towards development to prioritize uplifting the poorest citizens. As you engage in discussions with friends or family, the realization dawns that numbers like GDP and per capita income don't reveal the true story of prosperity. Instead, the essence of development lies in ensuring that every individual, regardless of their circumstances, has access to essential services like healthcare and education, reflecting the legacy of Gandhi’s vision. Thus, your commitment to supporting initiatives that promote social equity aligns with the core belief that for India to truly advance, it must elevate the lives of its most vulnerable people. 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

## **Critical Thinking** 

Key Point:The disparity between economic metrics and human well-being. 

Critical Interpretation:Kalam’s assertion that true development encompasses more than mere GDP growth challenges readers to question the adequacy of traditional economic indicators in measuring a nation's progress. While GDP may reflect economic activity, it often fails to account for inequality and overall quality of life. Critics argue that focusing solely on economic growth can exacerbate disparities and leave vulnerable populations behind, as shown in studies on income inequality (Piketty, 'Capital in the Twenty-First Century') or the limitations of GDP as a measure of welfare (Stiglitz, Sen, and Fitoussi Report). Readers should consider whether Kalam's perspective adequately addresses the multifaceted nature of development, rather than merely advocating for economic metrics.

## **Chapter 2: What Other Countries Envision for Themselves** 

## **Introduction** 

The chapter opens with a quote from the Buddha emphasizing critical thinking and self-analysis over blind acceptance of ideas from authority figures. A.P.J. Abdul Kalam asserts that India has yet to carve its own path and often imitates the success of other nations, losing faith in its unique capabilities. 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

## **Historical Context and Perception** 

Kalam reflects on India's historical contributions to technology and culture, noting that despite significant achievements, many Indians have developed a defeatist attitude, believing they cannot excel in their own innovations. He stresses the importance of recognizing India's past inventors and contributing to a belief in domestic capabilities. 

## **Learning from Global Visions** 

Kalam points out that while it is beneficial to learn from others, India must create its own vision. Observing how other countries like the USA, Europe, Malaysia, China, Japan, Korea, and Israel have framed strategic visions can offer valuable lessons. 

## **The USA and Europe** 

The United States, a major industrial and military power, developed its strength through ingenuity and an entrepreneurial spirit. Historical figures like Edison and Ford 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

exemplify the American commitment to long-term goals. The emphasis is on technology as a driver for economic growth and national strength. In contrast, no coherent long-term vision was found in the UK until recent governmental initiatives. Other European nations like Germany and France have taken steps to developed technological capabilities, also aligning their visions with market needs. 

## **Malaysia's Vision 2020** 

Malaysia has made significant strides economically by adopting a strategic master plan for becoming a fully developed nation by 2020. Under Prime Minister Mahathir Mohammed, Malaysia’s focus encompasses balanced development across various sectors, emphasizing local design capabilities and green policies. 

## **China's Strategic Goals** 

China aims to align itself with developed nations by promoting science and technology as central to its productivity. By 2020, it aims to bridge technological gaps in major industries and improve agricultural capabilities using advanced scientific techniques. 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

## **Japan's Technological Leadership** 

Japan exemplifies an organized long-term vision, having transformed its economy through diligent investment in technology and innovation. The country moved from being a net importer of technology to becoming a leading exporter, focusing on continuous improvement and upgrades in technological competencies. 

## **South Korea's Transformation** 

Once regarded as a poor nation, South Korea radically improved its economic status by embracing modern technologies in sectors including electronics and automotive industries. Focused investment in research and development allowed South Koreans to build formidable multinational corporations. 

## **Israel's Agricultural and Military Innovations** 

Israel emerged as a technological powerhouse despite its geographical challenges. By prioritizing water and food security, alongside defense capabilities, Israel developed

## advanced agricultural techniques and military technologies.

Kalam emphasizes that India is not alone in envisioning a future. Numerous countries are strategically thinking about technological advancements in various domains. The vision for India, shaped by its unique context and strengths, aspires to lift millions out of poverty and emerge as a developed nation by 2020.

## **Evolution of Technology Vision 2020** 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

## **India's Core Competencies** 

The pre-Independence era in India was marked by visionary leaders who aspired for a robust and modern nation. After gaining independence, India achieved progress across various sectors, including agriculture, healthcare, and education, defying expectations of chaos. However, over time, a shift towards dependency on foreign technology emerged, raising doubts about self-reliance. This led to the establishment of the Technology Information, Forecasting and Assessment Council (TIFAC) in 1988, aimed at forecasting emerging technologies for national development. 

## **Technology Vision 2020** 

In November 1993, the TIFAC governing council deliberated on transforming India's status from a developing nation to a developed one, emphasizing technology as a pivotal wealth generator. The discussions emphasized that technology could 

revolutionize education, agriculture, and infrastructure, **Install Bookey App to Unlock Full Text and** leading to the formation of task forces and panels dedicated **Audio** to realizing this vision.

## **Food, Agriculture and Processing** 

Today, approximately 4% of India's population lives below the poverty line, facing daily struggles to secure basic food. The current situation has improved from past famines and food shortages, particularly in the 1960s when India relied 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

heavily on American wheat imports. 

## **The Crisis and Indian Food Security** 

The mid-1960s food crisis epitomized India's precarious food security, prompting leadership to pursue self-sufficiency in food grains. The introduction of improved rice and wheat strains resulted in doubled wheat production within three years, leading to self-sufficiency in the 1970s. India now possesses a robust buffer stock of food grains and has diverse agricultural exports and processing activities. 

## **Future Needs and Capabilities** 

Despite progress, challenges remain. Projections suggest India may need to import up to 14 million tons of food grains by 2010 due to population growth and changing consumption patterns. Studies predict food demand could surge to between 191 and 286 million tons by 2000, with estimates of 340 million tons by 2020. The increasing demand for variety poses a strain on agriculture and food security. 

## **Challenges to Indian Agriculture** 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

The agricultural sector faces significant challenges, primarily from increasing demands for a variety of consumables, the reliance on irrigated land, and the need for sustainable techniques, especially in rain-fed areas which have traditionally lagged in agricultural advancements. Successful practices from other areas must be disseminated to tackle low productivity scenarios in eastern and central India. 

## **Environmental Problems and International Pressures** 

Global agricultural obligations under GATT and WTO compel India to modernize agricultural practices, improve product quality, and adopt environmental standards. The dependency on imports may lead to vulnerabilities, with foreign markets dictating terms. Additionally, agricultural practices must adapt to mitigate climate change impacts while maintaining food security. 

## **The Technologies** 

Advancements in biotechnology offer significant potential for crop production, particularly with transgenic plants, though current efforts primarily focus on pest-resistant 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

cotton. Research should emphasize key food crops, while conventional agricultural practices still hold importance. Water conservation and improved agricultural technologies will also be vital. 

## **Specific and Urgent Measures Needed** 

Proactive strategies are necessary to ensure food security, including expanding productive areas, diversifying crops, and enhancing productivity in underperforming regions. Rural and urban agricultural development must also consider socio-economic factors to encourage sustainable growth. 

## **Post-Harvest Technologies and Agro-Food Processing** 

The goal is for India to become a formidable player in global agriculture. This requires modernized post-harvest technologies to reduce waste and improve efficiency. Investment in storage and processing facilities is imperative to minimize losses and increase food availability. 

## **Vision for Action** 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

Achieving agricultural prosperity involves implementing immediate, medium, and long-term actions across various sectors—cereals, milk, fruits, and vegetables. A comprehensive action plan is critical to bolster India's agricultural sector and enhance food security for all citizens, creating resilience within local economies. 

In conclusion, realizing this vision requires persistent effort, strategic planning, and public engagement to foster a sustainable agricultural future for India that prioritizes food security and rural prosperity.

## **Chapter 5 Summary : Materials and the Future** 

## **Materials and the Future** 

In this chapter, A.P.J. Abdul Kalam discusses the critical role of materials in shaping human civilization and national development. The advances in material science have significantly influenced technology across diverse sectors, from aerospace to healthcare, and emphasize the importance of materials in everyday life. 

## **Role of Materials in Progress** 

Kalam highlights that agricultural products stem from biological resources, while non-living resources provide energy and various everyday chemicals. Materials like lightweight alloys have enabled technological advancements, aiding the development of aircraft, satellites, and medical devices. He underscores the significance of advanced materials in improving the quality of life, particularly for the disabled and aging population. 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

## **Vision for India's Material Landscape** 

Kalam presents a vision for India’s future in material science, acknowledging the strides made since independence but also the gaps that remain. He identifies 13 critical areas for development: steel, titanium, aluminum, rare earth materials, composites, ceramics, building materials, photonic materials, superconducting materials, polymeric materials, nuclear materials, biomaterials, and surface engineering. The engagement of R&D, industry, and academia is vital for achieving breakthroughs and acquiring competitive advantages. 

## **Strategic Resources and Development** 

The author discusses India’s rich mineral resources, particularly in iron, titanium, and aluminum, and emphasizes the need for effective use of these materials. He reflects on the missed opportunities in developing proprietary alloys and technologies due to a lack of collaboration among R&D institutions and industries. 

## **Industrial Growth Potential**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

Kalam outlines the potential growth of key industries such as steel, aluminum, and titanium by leveraging technological expertise and improving operational efficiencies. He calls for increased investments and innovative thinking to enhance India’s position in the global materials market.

## **Chapter 6 Summary : Chemicals Industries and Our Biological Wealth** 

**Chemical Industries and Our Biological Wealth** 

## **Introduction** 

The chapter discusses the significance and impact of chemical industries on human life, including both benefits and challenges. Chemicals play a crucial role in food security, health care, and everyday products. However, the industry also poses environmental risks such as pollution and accidents. 

## **The Dual Nature of Chemicals** 

While chemicals provide numerous advantages, they also introduce challenges like air and water pollution and the potential for catastrophic accidents, as exemplified by the Bhopal gas tragedy. The chapter highlights the need for ethical practices in chemical engineering to prevent misuse 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

and ensure safety. 

## **Growth and Development of the Chemical Industry** 

The chemical industry has evolved through distinct phases: penetration, consolidation, and specialty. It is deeply intertwined with economic, technological, and environmental considerations. The Indian chemical industry has seen growth since the establishment of its first petroleum refinery in 1954, currently forming a key part of the country’s industrial framework. 

## **Global Context and Challenges** 

India’s share of the global chemicals market is modest, yet the country has established a presence across various chemical sectors and possesses research and development capabilities. The industry must adapt to changing market dynamics, focus on sustainability, and overcome the reliance on imported technologies.

## **Chapter 7 Summary : Manufacturing for the future** 

## **MANUFACTURING FOR THE FUTURE** 

Manufacturing has evolved significantly over time, with India having a rich history of traditional skills and craftsmanship. Despite its innovative past, India lagged in adopting modern manufacturing technologies during the colonial era, only beginning to industrialize in earnest in the late 19th and early 20th centuries with some pioneering industries. 

## **THE MODERN FACE OF MANUFACTURING** 

The last 150 years have seen profound changes in manufacturing due to mass production techniques that originated in the West and were later advanced by Japan. The emergence of consumer demand transformed business paradigms, with the advent of electricity and electronics paving the way for computerized design and manufacturing processes like CAD and CAM. This evolution fosters 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

customization and improved efficiency in production methods. 

Innovative tools, including lasers and waterjets, have become essential in modern manufacturing, enabling operations that were previously impossible. The Indian manufacturing sector stands to benefit from the integration of computer technology, presenting opportunities to build on successes in software development, particularly in cities recognized as tech hubs like Bangalore and Hyderabad. 

## **TECHNOLOGY VISION FOR SOFTWARE** 

India must leverage its existing strengths in software development to capture emerging global markets, especially in advanced systems for manufacturing and healthcare. Projects already in motion highlight India's potential, but there’s an urgent need to improve the quality of software developed domestically to avoid dependency on foreign designs. The country can aspire to be a global player in high-end software production by 2020. 

## **OTHER MANUFACTURING SECTORS** 

While focusing on high-end manufacturing, a significant 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

portion of India’s economy is still supported by small and medium enterprises. These units need to enhance their operational efficiencies through modernization and upskilling as they face increasing competition from technologically advanced players. 

The vision for India also includes robust plans for specific industries, including textiles, electrical machines, and transportation equipment, each poised for substantial growth and innovation. 

## **ANYTHING UNIQUELY INDIAN?** 

India's progress in various manufacturing sectors raises questions about its unique contributions compared to global advancements. The ultimate goal should be to eradicate poverty and provide quality of life for all citizens while fostering a system that encourages innovation and adaptability. By addressing specific challenges in agriculture, chemical processes, and leveraging its biodiversity, India has the potential to create unique solutions and achieve global recognition, especially through advanced manufacturing technologies. 

In conclusion, India can strive for a balanced approach that incorporates both traditional strengths and modern 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

advancements, ensuring an inclusive and progressive future in manufacturing.

## **Chapter 8 Summary : Services As People's Wealth** 

**Services As People’s Wealth** 

## **Introduction to the Services Sector** 

The services sector has gained prominence in the economy as a significant component, encompassing various activities such as trading, marketing, public services, and teaching. The advancement in agriculture and manufacturing has accelerated its growth. In India, about 60-70% of the workforce is in agriculture, but as mechanization reduces agricultural jobs, many seek better opportunities in urban manufacturing. 

## **Interconnection of Sectors** 

Modern information technologies blur the lines between sectors, allowing for innovative services like computer simulations for product development or satellite data for 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

agricultural advisories. While some advocate that countries can thrive solely on services, Kalam argues that India must bolster its agriculture for food security and its manufacturing for economic stability. 

## **Role of Technology in Employment** 

The rise of technology, especially in computing, has revolutionized efficiency across sectors. However, the government and industries must prepare the workforce for continuous skill enhancement amid rapid technological advancements. 

## **India's Economic Landscape** 

In India, the agriculture sector contributes 32.7% to GDP, the manufacturing sector 25.8%, and the services sector approximately 40%. New areas such as advertising and consultancy are emerging, presenting opportunities for job creation and enhancing national wealth.

With proactive measures, the imagined future includes a well-connected, efficient services framework supporting economic growth for all Indians.

## **Chapter 9 Summary : Strategic Industries** 

## **Strategic Industries** 

## **Introduction to Strategy** 

- Strategy refers to planning and the conduct of campaigns in business, politics, or military contexts. 

- Strategic industries provide India with a competitive advantage beyond just military aspects. 

- Post-World War II, security encompasses economic and technological dimensions, along with traditional defense. 

## **Food Security** 

- Food availability is crucial for national well-being. 

- Maintaining consistent food supply against natural calamities is essential, especially for populous nations like India.

## **Economic Security** 

- Economic security is linked to sustainable growth and distribution of benefits. 

- Developed countries expand markets through multinational corporations and cartels, leading to high technological dependency for developing nations. 

- The balance between imports and exports is vital for economic stability, often pressure-laden and reliant on foreign investments. 

## **Critical Technologies for India** 

- India should focus on developing critical technologies to withstand economic instabilities. 

- The National Critical Technologies Panel identifies criteria for selecting crucial technologies for national competitiveness and security.

## **Chapter 10 Summary : Health Care for** 

## **All** 

## **Health Care for All** 

## **Introduction** 

The chapter emphasizes the essential need for universal health care in India, reflecting on the stark disparities in access to drinking water and health services among different socioeconomic classes. It asserts that a nation's progress can be measured by its ability to provide health care, which includes disease prevention and effective treatment. 

## **Disease Prevention** 

Effective sanitation, clean drinking water, and large-scale immunization programs are critical for preventing communicable diseases. The chapter highlights the importance of nutrition in disease prevention, noting that conditions like goiter and vitamin A deficiency could be 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

countered with simple dietary changes. Unfortunately, the poorer segments of society lack health education and access to medical resources. 

## **Sanitation and Drinking Water** 

The necessity for proper waste management and sanitation is outlined as pivotal for health. Case studies from urban slums illustrate the dire need for improved sanitation facilities. Innovative technologies, like those developed by DRDO for desalination, are proving effective in providing clean water to rural communities. 

## **Healthy Environments** 

For better health outcomes, environmental conditions, working conditions, and nutritional standards need to be prioritized. A robust health care system requires investments and proactive measures from employers and governments, supported by public awareness and community health services. 

## **Challenges of a Dual Society** 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

The chapter delineates the two starkly different 'Indias' that exist—the affluent populations that enjoy modern health care and amenities versus the majority who suffer from preventable diseases. A concerted effort is required to address diseases prevalent in developing countries, such as TB and AIDS, while also preparing for the rise of non-communicable diseases associated with modernization. 

## **Immediate Steps for Health Care** 

The chapter advocates for the urgent need to prioritize the eradication of infectious diseases and emphasizes research and development for preventive technologies. Community participation and awareness campaigns are crucial strategies for tackling health disparities. 

## **Non-communicable Diseases** 

A significant focus is placed on the rise of cardiovascular diseases and the cultural factors influencing health. It emphasizes the need for innovative, cost-effective diagnostic tools and treatments. The discussion covers diabetes and cancer, which also require a systemic response beyond specialized treatments limited to the wealthy. 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

## **Vision for the Future** 

The text concludes with a robust vision for a healthier India, stressing that efficient health care systems can be built by leveraging existing technologies and the capabilities of local industries. Maternal and child health are highlighted as crucial areas for development, with a call for a 

comprehensive approach that fosters improved health access for all segments of society. 

## **Conclusion** 

The overarching message is one of urgency and hope, calling for a collective effort to recognize health as a fundamental right, with particular emphasis on the interconnectedness of India's health ecosystem. The chapter highlights that providing care should not just be the responsibility of the government but a shared cause among all Indians, guided by empathy and concern for collective well-being. 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

Scan to Download 

## **Example** 

Key Point:The urgent need for universal health care in India 

Example:Imagine walking through your neighborhood, seeing a mother unable to afford vaccinations for her child while affluent families access the latest medical treatments without a thought. This stark reality exemplifies the urgent need for universal health care in India, where your community's health reflects the nation's progress. Ensuring equitable access to basic health services, sanitary conditions, and nutritional programs can transform lives and uplift society as a whole. 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

## **Critical Thinking** 

Key Point:Health as a Universal Right 

Critical Interpretation:While A.P.J. Abdul Kalam's call for universal health care reflects an aspirational vision for India, it also raises questions about the feasibility of such a system in a nation characterized by deep economic disparities. The expectation that health care should be universally accessible might underestimate the complexities involved in implementing policies that effectively bridge the gap between affluent and impoverished communities. Critics may argue that without addressing underlying issues of income inequality and governance, efforts toward universal health care could prove ineffective. This discussion is further supported by various studies, including reports from the World Health Organization, which underscore the significant challenges in ensuring equitable health services in developing countries.

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

## **Chapter 11 Summary : The Enabling Infrastructure** 

**The Enabling Infrastructure** 

## **Vision for Developed India** 

The vision of a developed India aims to eradicate poverty by 2010 and build a fast-growing economy leveraging core competencies across sectors. Agriculture, agro-food processing, and various fields necessitate engineered inputs, electronic sensors, and biotechnology to enhance productivity and connectivity. 

## **Interconnectedness and Trade** 

The effective supply of agricultural produce and value addition in sectors such as transportation is critical for competitiveness. This interconnectedness is vital for economic growth, requiring both physical and digital infrastructure to facilitate the swift movement of goods and 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

services. 

## **Technological Inputs and Value Addition** 

Value addition increases the market appeal of products, exemplified through innovative practices in agriculture, such as consistent quality packaging and preservation. Technological advancements can optimize supply chains, improve transportation, and ultimately enhance the consumer experience. 

## **Infrastructure Needs** 

Modern infrastructure, including roadways, railways, and ports, must be enhanced to enable economic growth. This requires significant investment, with estimates indicating a need for substantial funds to upgrade and expand existing infrastructure. 

## **Investment Dynamics** 

Investment in infrastructure has risen, yet both private and government sectors must collaborate to attract further investments while ensuring public interests. Innovations and 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

streamlined processes are necessary to reduce administrative bottlenecks hindering investment. 

## **Electric Power and Energy Needs** 

A robust electric power infrastructure is crucial for modernizing the economy. Current electricity generation and distribution systems face challenges, necessitating immediate actions to improve efficiency, reliability, and coverage. 

## **Renewable Energy Potential** 

Investments in renewable and clean energy sources, such as wind and solar, hold promise for sustainable development. Enhanced energy efficiency and conservation efforts are essential to maximize existing resources. 

## **Water Transport and Infrastructure** 

Waterways represent an efficient transportation method that is underutilized in India. Enhancing water transport could alleviate congestion in other transport systems and provide a cost-effective way of moving goods, especially in areas with navigable waterways. 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

## **Telecommunications as a Backbone** 

The telecommunications infrastructure plays a critical role in economic growth and connectivity. The government must encourage private sector participation to expand and improve the telecommunications network rapidly. 

## **Rural Connectivity and Development** 

Rural areas require modern facilities and connectivity to bridge the urban-rural divide, fostering economic growth and reducing urban congestion. Embedding decentralized industries could catalyze rural development. 

## **Technological Independence** 

India's achievements in technology often face external skepticism regarding the origin of innovations. There's a need to emphasize self-reliance in technological 

development, acknowledging indigenous contributions while dispelling misconceptions. 

This chapter highlights the intricacies of developing a robust infrastructure and emphasizes the need for a collaborative 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

approach between the government, private sector, and society to propel India towards becoming a developed nation.

## **Chapter 12 Summary : Realizing the** 

## **Vision** 

## **Realizing the Vision** 

## **Introduction to Vision and Challenges** 

A press conference held by a high-level committee revealed the common issue of reports without action in India. The shift in population dynamics, particularly with a younger demographic, signifies potential for actual progress towards the Technology Vision 2020 initiatives. The dismantling of outdated systems is seen as a catalyst for entrepreneurial growth. 

## **Restatement of the Vision** 

By 2020, India aims to be among the top five economic powers with self-reliance in national security, addressing agriculture, technology, and health sectors: 

- Agriculture: Enhance productivity and achieve

self-sufficient food production. 

- Technological Advancement: Develop capabilities in materials, pharmaceuticals, and clean processes. 

- Global Leadership: Strive for a significant services sector driven by skilled human resources.

A spirited discussion led to the realization that rather than providing a comprehensive action plan, individuals and sectors must contribute through incremental steps toward national development. Emphasizing the role of “punyatmas,” “punyadhikaris,” and “punyanetas,” collective action is encouraged.

## **India 2020 Questions** View on Bookey Website 

## **Chapter 1 | Can India Become a Developed Country?| Q&A** 

## **1.Question** 

## **What is a key indicator of whether India can become a developed nation?** 

Answer:A key indicator is the per capita income, 

which reflects the average income of people, but does not show the distribution of wealth among the population. 

## **2.Question** 

## **What is the significance of Gandhi's talisman in the context of development?** 

Answer:Gandhi's talisman states that every action should 

wipe tears from the face of the poorest person. This implies that true development can only be measured by the welfare of the most disadvantaged. 

## **3.Question** 

## **How does technological advancement contribute to** 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

## **India's vision of development?** 

Answer:Technological advancement is crucial as it leads to economic growth, creates productive employment, and improves living standards, making it integral for India’s aspirations to develop. 

## **4.Question** 

## **What major challenge does India face on the path to** 

## **becoming a developed country?** 

Answer:India faces challenges such as income inequality and ensuring that the benefits of economic growth are equitably distributed among all its citizens. 

**5.Question** 

**According to the author, how can India ensure its national security in an economically dominated world?** Answer:India must develop indigenous technologies to strengthen its defenses and ensure economic independence, as reliance on external technologies undermines national security. 

**6.Question** 

**What is the vision for India's economy by the year 2020,** 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

## **as laid out in the text?** 

Answer:By 2020, India aims to be among the top economies globally, ensuring high standards of education, health, and security for its citizens while maintaining sustainable growth. 

**7.Question** 

## **What does the author imply by saying 'a nation is made great by its people'?** 

Answer:This suggests that the true strength of a nation lies in the empowerment, education, and well-being of its people, which ultimately shapes its development. 

## **8.Question** 

## **How can social opportunities play a role in India's growth according to Amartya Sen?** 

Answer:Expansion of educational facilities, healthcare, and social security are essential for creating social opportunities that enable individuals to improve their living conditions and contribute to economic growth. 

**9.Question** 

## **What critical infrastructure development does the author emphasize for achieving a developed India?**

**Why is a long-term perspective important for India's vision of development?** Answer:A long-term perspective ensures that the progress achieved is sustainable and that all citizens can benefit from ongoing economic and social advancements. 

**Chapter 2 | What other countries Envision for Themselves| Q&A** 

**1.Question** 

**What is the role of belief in achieving excellence according to A.P.J. Abdul Kalam?** Answer:A.P.J. Abdul Kalam emphasizes the importance of critical thinking and independent belief over blind faith in tradition or authority. He refers to a teaching by the Buddha, advocating for individuals to believe only in doctrines that contribute positively to the welfare of all being. This 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

approach is crucial for fostering excellence, as it 

urges people to analyze, question, and innovate rather than merely imitate. 

**2.Question** 

**How can India regain its confidence in technology and innovation?** Answer:Kalam suggests overcoming the defeatist mentality that has crept into Indian society by reviving the faith in indigenous capabilities. He highlights the importance of looking at India's past accomplishments in technology, such as the invention of rockets by Tippu Sultan's engineers. By recognizing and celebrating these achievements, India can inspire a new generation to innovate and contribute to global technological advancements. 

**3.Question** 

**What vision did the United States establish to drive its technological growth?** Answer:The United States adopted a comprehensive vision aimed at achieving technological leadership through strategic 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

commitments from both the government and private sectors. This vision included significant investment in research and development, promoting innovation across industries, and fostering a competitive spirit that enabled individuals and corporations to thrive and push technological boundaries. 

**4.Question** 

**Can you explain the significance of Japan’s technological strategy?** 

Answer:Japan's strategy was built on the principle of developing internal technological capabilities rather than solely relying on importing technology. This was achieved through sustained investments in R&D and collaboration among various sectors, leading Japan to transform from a technology importer to a net exporter. Their long-term vision incorporated collective effort from industries, consumers, and academics, demonstrating the power of unified goals in achieving national advancements. 

**5.Question** 

**In what ways did Malaysia’s vision for the future resemble India’s aspirations?** 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

Answer:Both Malaysia and India recognized the importance of a cohesive national vision for development. Malaysia's Technology Vision 2020 emphasized balanced growth in economic, social, and environmental sectors. Similarly, India’s vision entails a holistic approach to addressing poverty and technological advancement. Both countries aspire to enhance indigenous capabilities and promote sustainable practices while leveraging foreign investments. **6.Question What lessons can India learn from Israel’s approach to agriculture and technology?** Answer:Israel’s approach underscores the value of innovation and technology in overcoming natural resource constraints. Despite its geographical challenges, Israel prioritized food security and pursued advances in agriculture, achieving remarkable productivity. India can emulate this model by developing and implementing sustainable agricultural practices and leveraging technology to enhance food security and crop yield.

## **7.Question** 

**How does A.P.J. Abdul Kalam view the relationship between technological development and national identity?** Answer:Kalam views technological development as intrinsic to a nation's identity and progress. He argues that developing indigenous technologies fosters self-reliance and national pride, allowing countries like India to navigate global challenges effectively. By establishing a strong tech base, a nation not only secures its future but also gains a significant standing on the international stage. 

**8.Question** 

**What is the common feature shared by the nations discussed regarding technological vision?** Answer:The common feature among the nations is the establishment of a clear,long-term vision that integrates science and technology into economic growth strategies. Each country's approach emphasizes the necessity of research and development, innovation, and the collaboration of various sectors to drive national progress and address societal

## needs. 

## **9.Question** 

**What is Kalam’s call to action for India as it looks toward 2020?** 

Answer:Kalam calls for India to eradicate poverty and establish itself as a developed nation by 2020. He urges the nation to harness its technological strengths and leverage innovation as a means of achieving economic prosperity and societal welfare. His message is one of belief in India's potential and the need to take affirmative, collective action to realize a brighter future.

It includes being able to absorb new techniques and skills effectively, and accomplish better outcomes given the same effort, often due to a supportive environment or unique experiences. 

**3.Question**

## **Chapter 4 | Food, Agriculture and Processing| Q&A** 

**1.Question** 

**What historical context led to India's current food self-sufficiency?** Answer:In the years 1965-67, India faced severe food shortages, depending on foreign grain imports during droughts. This crisis prompted the leadership to prioritize self-sufficiency in food grains, coinciding with advancements in agricultural technology, particularly in high-yield varieties of wheat and rice. Consequently, within a short period, India's wheat production doubled, leading to food grain self-sufficiency by the 1970s. 

**2.Question** 

**What challenges does India face regarding food and agriculture in the future?** Answer:India's agricultural challenges include a rising population projected at 1.3 billion by 2020, increased economic growth leading to higher consumption, changes in 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

dietary habits, and the need to efficiently manage limited arable land while maintaining sustainable practices. 

**3.Question** 

**How can technological advancements play a role in enhancing agricultural productivity in India?** Answer:Technological advancements, including biotechnology for developing pest-resistant crops, efficient irrigation methods, remote sensing for monitoring crop yields, and improved post-harvest processing technologies, are crucial for increasing productivity and ensuring food security in India. 

**4.Question** 

**What is the vision for India's agricultural future as expressed in this chapter?** Answer:The vision for India's agricultural future emphasizes becoming a global leader in agricultural production, focusing on sustainable practices, improving yields in rain-fed areas, leveraging technology to enhance food processing, and ensuring food security for the population while addressing 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

climate change challenges. 

**5.Question** 

**Why is post-harvest technology critical for India’s agricultural success?** Answer:Post-harvest technology is critical as it significantly reduces food losses, currently estimated at 10% for grains and 25% for fruits and vegetables. Modernizing storage and processing facilities will not only minimize wastage but also enhance the economic value of agricultural products, contributing to rural prosperity. 

**6.Question What lessons from the Green Revolution can be applied to future agricultural strategies in India?** Answer:The Green Revolution demonstrated the importance of adopting new technologies, extending knowledge to farmers, and fostering community-driven agricultural practices. Future strategies should replicate these principles by focusing on region-specific crops, enhancing farmer education, and promoting agricultural innovation through 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

collaboration across disciplines. 

**7.Question** 

**What specific actions are recommended to improve water management for agricultural sustainability?** Answer:The chapter emphasizes improving water-use efficiency through methods like drip irrigation and rainwater harvesting, treating water as a national resource, and implementing modern irrigation techniques to support agriculture in arid regions, ensuring that water scarcity does not limit agricultural productivity. 

**8.Question** 

**What role do international standards and obligations play in shaping India’s agricultural practices?** Answer:International trade agreements and environmental standards necessitate compliance with safety and quality measures that dictate the use of agrochemicals and fertilizers. This shapes India's agricultural practices and compels adherence to sustainable methods, aligning domestic farming with global markets. 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

## **9.Question** 

**How does the chapter propose improving agricultural practices in rain-fed regions?** 

Answer:The chapter suggests focusing on region-specific crops suited to local agro-climatic conditions, enhancing community-based water conservation efforts, and providing farmers in rain-fed areas with exposure to successful agricultural models from more productive regions. 

**10.Question** 

**What is the importance of farmer education and innovation according to the chapter?** Answer:Educating farmers about modern agricultural practices, technologies, and market demands is vital for increasing productivity and sustainability. Promotion of innovation through interaction, exposure to advanced techniques, and fostering entrepreneurship among farmers can lead to significant agricultural improvements. 

**Chapter 5 | Materials and the Future| Q&A**

Their pervasive use—from prosthetics that assist disabled individuals to materials in technological 

devices—illustrates their role in everyday comforts, enhanced accessibility, and improved health outcomes.

Mastery of high-tech materials can lead to competitive advantages in industries like aerospace, healthcare, and construction, resulting in economic growth and employment generation. As global markets evolve, embracing technology enables India to emerge as a key player rather than a subordinate exporter. **4.Question**

**5.Question**

**7.Question**

## **2.Question** 

**What role does infrastructure play in economic connectivity and growth?** Answer:Infrastructure is pivotal for economic connectivity as it facilitates the movement of goods and services across different regions. For example, efficient transport networks (roadways, railways, waterways, and air transport) and communication systems enable timely delivery of products, reducing wastage and improving overall productivity. When production centers are interconnected, it allows for value addition and enables the economy to thrive. 

**3.Question** 

**Why is value addition important in the production process?** Answer:Value addition enhances the worth of a raw product through processes such as sorting, packaging, and branding, making it more appealing to consumers. For instance, consistently delivering high-quality mangoes that meet consumer expectations can justify higher prices. This not 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

only boosts farmers' incomes but also strengthens the overall economy by maximizing the potential of locally produced goods. 

## **4.Question** 

## **How can technological advancements improve India’s infrastructure?** 

Answer:Technological advancements such as computerized inventory systems, electronic weighing machines, and automated clearing processes can significantly improve India’s infrastructure, specifically in transportation and logistics. These technologies minimize downtime, enhance efficiency, and ensure the quality of goods during transit, which ultimately leads to better service delivery and economic growth. 

## **5.Question** 

## **What is the significance of telecommunication and information technology in creating an enabling** 

## **infrastructure?** 

Answer:Telecommunication and information technology are crucial as they allow for the instant transfer of information

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

essential for business operations. A robust communication network enhances logistics, improves service delivery, and supports innovations across various sectors. Investing in telecom infrastructure fosters economic development by connecting rural and urban areas and facilitating access to information and services. 

**6.Question** 

**How does energy supply impact economic development?** Answer:Quality electric power is fundamental to the functioning of modern economies. Interruptions or inefficiencies in power supply can impede industrial operations, lower productivity, and deter investments. By ensuring consistent and quality electricity supply, India can enhance operational efficiency across all sectors and provide a conducive environment for economic growth. 

**7.Question** 

**What strategies can be implemented to improve India’s power generation capacity?** Answer:To improve power generation capacity, India should 

**==> picture [370 x 66] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [61 x 60] intentionally omitted <==**

**Scan to Download** 

invest in renewable energy sources, enhance existing infrastructure for better transmission, and increase efficiency across power plants. Focusing on smaller, decentralized power projects and refurbishing old stations can also yield immediate results. Additionally, promoting nuclear power and exploring cleaner technologies will be essential for sustainable growth. 

**8.Question** 

**In what way can India's rural areas benefit from improved infrastructure?** 

Answer:Improved infrastructure in rural areas can facilitate better access to markets, education, and healthcare, reducing migration to urban centers. Establishing efficient transport systems and reliable energy sources can encourage industries to set up in these regions, enhancing local economies and providing livelihoods, which in turn can lead to a more balanced and inclusive national development. 

**9.Question** 

**What is the vision for renewable energy sources in India and their potential impact?**

Answer:The vision for renewable energy sources like biomass, wind, and solar is to reduce dependency on fossil fuels and ensure sustainable energy supply. By harnessing these technologies, India can empower underserved regions with energy, promote economic development, and mitigate environmental impacts. This not only addresses the immediate energy needs but also contributes to long-term ecological sustainability. 

**10.Question** 

**How can India leverage its technological advancements to become an IT superpower?** Answer:India can leverage its technological advancements by enhancing IT education, promoting software development, and fostering an entrepreneurial ecosystem. By removing bureaucratic hurdles and encouraging private sector participation, India can create a conducive environment for IT innovation and expand its presence in the global market, thus becoming a significant player in the global IT landscape. **Chapter 12 | Realizing the Vision| Q&A**

## PENGUIN BOOKS 

## IGNITED MINDS 

A.P.J. Abdul Kalam is one of India’s most distinguished scientists. He was responsible for the development of India’s first satellite launch vehicle, the SLV3, and the development and operationalization of strategic missiles. As chairman of the Technology Information, Forecasting and Assessment Council, he pioneered India Vision 2020, a roadmap for transforming India into an economically developed nation by 2020, focusing on PURA as a development system for countrywide implementation. 

Kalam held various positions in the Indian Space Research Organisation and the Defence Research and Development Organisation and became principal scientific adviser to the Government of India, holding the rank of a cabinet minister. 

The President of India between 2002 and 2007, Kalam has been awarded honorary doctorates from thirty-eight universities and the country’s three highest civilian honours—Padma Bhushan (1981), Padma Vibhushan (1990) and Bharat Ratna (1997). 

Kalam has authored fifteen books on a variety of topics that have been translated into many languages across the world. His most significant works are _Wings of Fire, India 2020: A Vision for the New Millennium_ and _Ignited Minds._ He now conducts lectures on societal development in many international institutes and is involved in research on different societal missions. 

## PRAISE FOR THE BOOK 

‘The strength of _Ignited Minds …_ lies in the scientist’s ability to present some of the most tangled issues that confront the nation in easily digestible packages of information.’ 

–Raj Chengappa in _India Today_ 

‘Kalam is a dreamer of great dreams … _Ignited Minds_ will fire the minds of the young to whom it is primarily addressed.’ 

–Khushwant Singh in _Outlook_ 

‘It is not possible in [a] short review to convey the vast erudition of a man who covers so much of India’s past, present and future in such simple, communicable terms. Or his many revolutionary as well as practical ideas for the country which he so obviously loves, of which he is so proud and which he feels can be the country of his dreams by 2020.’ 

–Amita Malik in _Hindu_ 

‘ _Ignited Minds_ is a book to be read by the leaders and led, by young as well as old and by all who love their country … Kalam[dares] to say what has long needed to be said but which has gone unsaid …’ 

–M.V. Kamath in _Sentinel_ 

‘ _Ignited Minds_ is a lucid and elegant expression of [Kalam’s] dreams about India’s future … This is a feel-good book. It is a true patriot’s self-help guide to a better nation.’ 

–Wilson John in _Pioneer_ 

_I dedicate this book to a child who is studying in class 12. Her name is Snehal Thakkar. On 11 April 2002 when I reached Anand by road in the evening, it was under curfew following communal disturbances. The next day, at the Anandalaya High School, while talking to the students, a question came up: ‘Who is our enemy?’_ 

_There were many answers, but the one we all agreed was correct came from her: ‘Our enemy is Poverty.’_ 

_It is the root cause of our problems and should be the object of our fight, not our own._ 

## Preface 

Nations consist of people. And with their effort a nation can accomplish all it could ever want. Motivating India’s people, and its youth especially, is the central theme of _Ignited Minds,_ which continues the trajectory of thoughts taken up in my earlier two books, _Wings of Fire_ and _India 2020: A Vision for the New Millennium,_ written with my friends Arun Tiwari and Y.S. Rajan. I have chosen to write about this subject of igniting young minds so that India turns into a developed nation by the year 2020 because all through my career in the field of technology and its management, I relied on the power and potential of youth. My strength has been my young teams who never let me down. And what satisfaction there was in working with them on some of the most complex projects in some of the most challenging situations! Given the freedom to achieve and guided properly, I am convinced the young of India can accomplish far more. 

As I began writing, I wondered if I was not overreaching myself. I thought: Who am I to write about this capacity of India to realize its destiny as a developed nation? What do I really know about how this can be accomplished beyond what I have learned in my projects and missions evolved around science and technology? Isn’t this an area that political leaders, economists, thinkers and other competent people would address better? How am I qualified to tell others about an ability that has been generally ignored? 

At first as I was putting down my experiences with youth, I had no idea of what I would have to say. However, I put aside my doubts and began to examine what I hear from the people I meet during my visits to different places, particularly children, saints and seers, teachers, scientists, industry leaders and even political leaders. I am sure on my part that India has the ability to transform itself into a developed nation. Through my projects in space, defence and nuclear sectors, I know that our people have the ability to achieve the best in the world. 

They have a fantastic mix of belief and knowledge that sets them apart from any other nation on earth. I also know that their potential has gone untapped because we have become used to being subjugated and docile. What better project can I undertake than to tell my people that what they dream of can become possible, that they can have anything that comprises a good life: health, education, the freedom to pursue their goals, and above all, peace. 

My quest for answers as to how this could be done took me to schools, the countryside, ashrams and many other places which were not part of my itinerary earlier. It was a new kind of experience, a very stimulating one at that. The paddy fields in Bihar left to an ad-hoc cycle of agriculture, the untapped mineral wealth of the newly formed state of Jharkhand and the unattended biodiversity of Tripura are throwing a great challenge to the knowledge era that is dawning. In Assam the sight of the mighty Brahmaputra almost mesmerized me. Its vast expanse of water filled me with a strange sense of helplessness too–the river’s untapped flow was taking a gigantic mass of water into the sea. It made me think, that as a nation too we were failing to utilize our tremendous energies. 

Where are we making a mistake? What is it that needs to be corrected? We have a roadmap in our five-year plans that covers some of the things we need to achieve. We have most of the necessary resources. There seems to be an attitude problem, as if we cannot shake ourselves out of a mindset of limited achievement. This book is all about breaking away from the forces that would prefer us to remain a nation of a billion people selling cheap labour and raw materials and providing a large market for goods and services of other nations. 

I am writing this book to make my young readers hear a voice that says, ‘Start moving.’ Leadership must lead us to prosperity. Young Indians with constructive ideas should not have to see them wither in the long wait for approval. They have to rise above norms which are meant to keep them timid in the name of safety and to discourage entrepreneurship in the name of trade regimes, organizational order and group behaviour. As it is said, Thinking is the capital, Enterprise is the way, Hard Work is the solution. 

Every nation has struggled to achieve its goals. Generations have given their best to make life better for their offspring. There is nothing mysterious or hidden about this, no alternative to effort. And yet we fail to follow the winning track. More than the problems outside–globalization, recession, inflation, insurgency, 

instability and so on–I am concerned about the inertia that has gripped the national psyche, the mindset of defeat. I believe that when we believe in our goals, that what we dream of can become reality, results will begin to follow. _Ignited Minds_ is about developing that conviction in ourselves, and discarding the things that hold us back. 

This was, in fact, a central thought that I kept in mind as I wrote. Share my dream of a developed India and see it made real in your own and others’ lives. In my own way, I have tried to follow my beliefs, to do what I loved doing. I have tried, however, to guide but not to impose my views on others. 

You will find in this book plain speaking: Surge ahead as a developed nation or perish in perpetual poverty, subservient to a few countries that control the world politically and economically. There are no other alternatives. 

In the nine chapters of this book, I take up various themes. I begin with a rumination on peace, without which there can be no progress, and on the shift in the direction of my own life that occurred after surviving a helicopter crash. There is a chapter based on my interaction with children all over India. Other chapters contain the insights I gained in my meetings with saints and seers, scientists, outstanding thinkers and others. There are accounts of some promising experiments in agriculture and in the medical field. Elsewhere I deal with concepts that carry the seed of solutions. The contents essentially come from the people of this nation, from what they have taught me. 

I have written this book as an expression of my faith in the potential of India and my countrymen. We have all the resources we need, whether it be people, talent, natural bounty or other assets. India is truly blessed with a real, though latent, abundance. Scarcity of resources is not the cause of our problems. Our problems originate in our approach towards them. We are spreading our resources too wide and too thin. With our resources and the money we spend we could easily accomplish three times what we do, in half the time we normally take, if we were to operate in mission mode with a vision for the nation. The vision generates the best in every field. 

We must change tracks. It is imperative that our policy making become more responsive and efficient so that the stifled entrepreneurship is liberated. Key to that is better coordination among the various departments, rather than emphasis on priorities according to the preferences of individual departments. There are 

more reviews than views available. Every channel appears blocked by some obstacle or the other. The trapped energies and the suppressed initiative need to be freed and properly harnessed. Nor do we particularly need every time to borrow models from elsewhere. I don’t think the American, Japanese or Singaporean solutions will work for us. Knocking at others’ doors will be futile. Instead of importing theories and transplanting concepts we need to grow our own solutions. Instead of searching for answers outside we will have to look within for them. 

I hope that when you go through these nine chapters you will be given the guidance that I got from the people of my country and feel connected to the wisdom that is so special to this soil. The reality of a developed nation will become part of your daily life. Twenty years from now I may not be around. But I am sure many of you will be there to share in the glory of success and agree that I was right in being so confident. 

Many friends and associates helped me put this book together. I am grateful to them all. My special thanks to Mr Y.S. Rajan, and Dr M.S. Vijayaraghavan for shaping my thoughts with their vital inputs. Dr A. Sivathanu Pillai has worked with me for a long time and his contribution has been both timely and invaluable in giving shape to ideas and thoughts. I am fortunate to have his friendship. I am grateful to Mr H. Sheridon who directly keyed in my dictations into his laptop computer with outstanding skill. My co-author in _Wings of Fire,_ Mr Arun K. Tiwari, did his usual craftsmanship with words on the manuscript and I appreciate every bit of that. It was a great pleasure to work with Mr Krishan Chopra of Penguin Books. From the emanation of my thoughts to the book’s realization, his constant interaction was of great support. 

## **Chennai April 2002** 

_**A.P.J. Abdul Kalam**_ 

1 

## The Dream and the Message 

_Dream, Dream, Dream Dream transform into thoughts And thoughts result in action_ 

On 30 September 2001, I was on my way to Bokaro from Ranchi in Jharkhand when the helicopter carrying me crashed moments before landing. It hit the earth with a thud after its engine failed. All of us on board had a miraculous escape. Grateful to God but unfazed by the incident, I went ahead with my scheduled programme of addressing the students in Bokaro. At night, however, a panel of doctors persuaded me to take a tranquillizer to alleviate my perceived shock. The drug made me sleep hours ahead of my usual time–1 a.m. I also failed to rise at my usual 6 a.m. and woke up only after eight o’clock. 

It was, however, a disturbed sleep, and sometime in the middle of it, I fell to thinking why the human race, the best of all of God’s creations, has been so deeply divided by violence. I imagined a conversation between five people who together symbolize the finest attributes of the human mind and whom I admire deeply. Through their conversation, I sought an answer. In this experience, much more intense and vivid than a dream, though for want of a better word I shall term it that, I saw myself in a desert with miles of sand all around. There was a full moon and the desert was bathed in its light. Five men– Mahatma Gandhi, Albert Einstein, Emperor Asoka, Abraham Lincoln and Caliph Omar– stood in a circle, their clothes ruffled by the wind. 

I felt myself dwarfed standing next to the majestic Emperor Asoka. Asoka led two lives, one as a ruthless conqueror and the other as a compassionate ruler. The man I stood beside was the one who had just returned from conquest. But victory had been obtained at heavy cost: the battle of Kalinga claimed the lives 

of at least 300,000 people and an equal number were wounded. I saw everyone looking at Asoka who fell on his knees and removed his armour and crown. His face was pale, reflecting the death surrounding him. He looked at the sky. He saw the bright cool moon shining and God’s grace pouring down on mother earth. And he looked down at the horror he had created, making blood flow everywhere. In that moment of beauty and horror–the silver moonlight and the suffering and pain on the ground, when Nature itself seemed to speak out against what he had wrought, Ahimsa Dharma was born. Emperor Asoka embraced God’s command to propagate love for human beings through this doctrine. 

As I stood by, I wondered. Why the Kalinga war, why the assassination of Mahatma Gandhi and of Abraham Lincoln? Or many others like them? Has God Almighty faltered in His Creation? Is the destruction of mankind essential for a Second Creation? 

In that blissful silence the Mahatma spoke, ‘Friends, the divine message we are hearing is the message of creation. Since we all belong to planet earth, we may give a message to mankind, how people of different races, religions and languages can live peacefully and prosperously together. 

‘God Almighty has blessed us all with something unique that we passed on to mankind through our deeds and efforts. Is that working? Is there any divine message or doctrine? Divine beauty should enter the human soul and happiness blossom in the body and mind. Is it possible?’ 

Asoka said, ‘Friends, there is one thing I have realized, there is no victory in causing suffering. Triumph is a peaceful kingdom.’ 

Caliph Omar said, ‘I learned after I entered Jerusalem that all men are equal. There is no point in forcing others to follow your path. You will get only that which is ordained for you. God alone is the sovereign.’ 

Caliph Omar never saw his position in terms of the special privileges that it carried. To him government was a sacred trust and he did his best not to betray that trust in any way. 

It was Einstein’s turn. ‘I would like to recall my friend Werner Heisenberg’s view, “You know, in the West we have built a large, beautiful ship. It has all the comforts in it, but one thing is missing: it has no compass and does not know where to go. Men like Tagore and Gandhi and their spiritual forebears found the 

compass. Why can this compass not be put in the human ship so that both can realize their purpose?”’ 

Abraham Lincoln, the great American leader who fought against slavery and whose life paralleled that of the Mahatma in certain respects, said at this point, ‘There is one thing that I would like to say: happiness comes from a family’s prosperity at various levels. God’s grace gives bliss to human lives. Happiness and bliss are two important components of a godly life on earth. Perhaps there is so much conflict between peoples and nations because in our pursuit of prosperity and power we have lost sight of ethical values. We must ask ourselves, what is the role of human consciousness? Does it have a part in political thinking, scientific thinking and theological thinking? Is spirituality acceptable in the business of life?’ 

Mahatma Gandhi recalled sage Ashtavakra who propounded, ‘“Oh my son! You are the very Consciousness within which arises this phenomenal universe that is not separate from what you are. How can there be a question of anything being acceptable or unacceptable?” Let the business of life be peace and prosperity, and not exploitation and conflict. 

‘This is our message to the planet. Everything that we do, any doctrine that we espouse, should be for the good of humankind.’ 

The next morning I kept sitting for some time drinking my tea and pondering about this strange dream. What if the helicopter had lost power at some more height? Just a few hours before my own mishap, a plane carrying a promising leader and a team of young and talented journalists had crashed, killing all. I had been lucky to survive and now there was the night’s experience that seemed to hold a message for me. What should I do? 

I looked out of the window. The sun was well up in the sky and there was a soothing breeze. I have always lived in close touch with nature and have always found it a friend, giving without reservation, like the mango tree–people throw stones at it, break off its branches, but it still offers its shade to the weary traveller, and its fruit to the hungry. Whether it was the sea at Rameswaram, Thumba and Chandipur; the desert at Pokhran; or the gigantic boulders in Hyderabad, nature has always made its presence felt wherever I have worked. It has helped to remind me of the divine force that pervades all of creation. 

I kept on pondering over my dream. And yet, the history of the world shows the forces of good struggling hard to make life better for mankind while the human race also shows a terrible capacity for destruction. Thus we have Gandhi, and other great saints and teachers who lay down the precepts for a happy and virtuous life, on the one hand, and on the other the death of millions in the Second World War and the dropping of atomic bombs that destroyed entire cities. Thousands have died in the Bosnia conflict, the Israel—Palestine conflict continues to take lives, and on 11 September 2001 terrorists used a new tactic to take lives when they struck at the World Trade Center in New York. At home, in the Bhopal gas tragedy, 30,000 people died as the result of the carelessness of a multinational company, and thousands more have died in the Kashmir Valley violence. On 13 December 2001, when the leaders of India were in Parliament, an attempt was made by the terrorists to paralyse the country. Where will it all stop? Are we doomed to destroy ourselves? No, we have to find an everlasting solution. 

I recall a poem I wrote a few years ago, ‘The Tree of Life’. 

‘You, the human race are the best of my creations You will live and live, And give and give till you are united, In happiness and pain! My bliss will be born in you, Love is a continuum, That is the mission of humanity, You will see every day in the Life Tree. You learn and learn, My best of creations.’ 

The five great human beings I saw in my dream lived at different times. In the modern world, there are few examples of human beings who embody the qualities that come from realizing the nature of the mind. Once a child asked me if I had read the Mahabharata and if so, who my favourite character in it was. The multifaceted characters in the epic represent almost every aspect of human nature, good as well as bad. I told the child that I was particularly attracted to the character of Vidura, who showed grit against the wrongdoings of authority and had the courage to differ when everyone else chose to surrender before the tyranny of adharma. 

Today, it is hard for us to find one true Vidura among our leaders. It is hard for us to imagine such an enlightened being and even harder for us to aim for such enlightenment. More discouraging still is the quality of public life today, the low level of discourse and the presence of so much ego, anger, greed, jealousy, spite, cruelty, lust, fear, anxiety and turmoil! I felt a new determination dawning inside me. 

In this my most important decision I decided to help discover the nature of India’s true self in its children. My own work and indeed I as a person were relegated to the background. My scientific career, my teams, my awards, all this became secondary. I wanted instead to be a part of the eternal intelligence that is India. I hoped to transcend myself and discover the inner, higher self that is in us through my interaction with joyous children. 

A man is said to pass through different stages in his lifetime. Dr Wayne W. Dyer, in his book _Manifest Your Destiny_ , makes an interesting categorization of them as athelete stage, warrior stage, statesperson stage and spirit stage. It occurred to me that nations too make a similar transition and in extending this analogy to them I have termed the last two stages big brother and selfrealization stages respectively. The stages do not follow in sequence necessarily; they can be coexistent, with one aspect dominant.
